title: Reading News
intro: What's new from the authors topping your reading logs — fresh releases, a few read-alikes, and where to take a keen reader next.

items.headline: Michael Morpurgo has a busy 2026 — including a memoir of life on the farm
items.author: Michael Morpurgo
items.summary: The author of Running Wild has several titles due in 2026, among them a memoir of his fifty years on a working Devon farm and more retellings of Shakespeare for younger readers. Good news for a class already hooked on his animal stories.

items.headline: The Roman Mysteries author is retelling Virgil for younger readers
items.author: Caroline Lawrence
items.summary: Caroline Lawrence, whose Roman Mysteries your readers enjoy, has been writing a set of short, accessible Tales from Virgil's Aeneid. A natural next step for a child who's caught the Ancient Rome bug.

items.headline: More gory-fun history from the Horrible Histories author
items.author: Terry Deary
items.summary: Terry Deary's Terrible True Tales bring the same grisly humour as Horrible Histories to newly-confident readers, in shorter illustrated stories. Handy for a child who loves the facts but wants a lighter read.

items.headline: Why the Worst Witch keeps topping the reading logs
items.author: Jill Murphy
items.book: A Bad Spell for the Worst Witch
items.summary: Mildred Hubble's mishaps at Miss Cackle's Academy have charmed readers since 1974, and they're your most-read series right now. Children who race through the eight books can carry on with the CBBC television series.

items.headline: Biff, Chip and Kipper — a watch-alike for your earliest readers
items.author: Roderick Hunt
items.summary: The Oxford Reading Tree trio your beginner readers meet have their own screen outings — the CBeebies series Biff & Chip and the classic Magic Key animation — worth pointing families to for a little reading support at home.
